focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

Gavin hadn't slept. The Astronomy Tower was the coldest place in the castle and he hadn't brought a cloak.

The stone floor leached heat through his robes as he leaned back against the parapet and pulled the cloth tight around his chest. Wind came through the gaps between the crenellations in bursts that flattened his hair and then died and came again. He'd been up here for a while. An hour, maybe. Long enough that his hands had stopped just being cold and had moved into the numb stage where the fingers didn't bend right when he tried to make a fist.

March in Scotland. The thaw had started but hadn't committed — patches of snow still on the mountains, the grounds mostly mud with green pushing through in places, the lake half-frozen and half-not in a way that looked like a decision the water couldn't make. From up here the Forbidden Forest was a dark shape pressed against the hills, and below it the lawns sloped down to Hagrid's cabin, which was dark. Hagrid might be in the forest tonight. Gavin didn't know. He hadn't paid attention to Hagrid's schedule in months. He hadn't paid attention to most things in months.

He'd come up because the sub-collection was too small. The walls of those cut-stone rooms had started to press. Fourteen weeks of working there — the limestone smell in his clothes, the faint glow of stones that predated everything else in the castle — and the air had begun to feel used. After he'd lain on his bed with the manuscript until the notation blurred into shapes, he'd shoved it back into his trunk and kept walking. Out of the dormitory, out of the common room, up the stairs past the library landing. Up more stairs. Past the Dark Arts classroom, dark now, the door closed. Up again. The Astronomy Tower because it was the highest place he...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...hen the room tightened.

The round window clicked in its lead frame. Every hair on his forearms lifted. The wall sconce opposite his bed thinned from a steady amber tongue to a blue needle edged in orange. The change was small, maybe two seconds, but the whole dormitory felt narrower in that interval, as if the stone had drawn half an inch closer to his ribs. On the bed the altered quill lost another degree of itself. The shaft did not just blur. Its middle seemed to go missing, nib and vane hanging in the air with barely enough quill between them.

Then the pressure eased. The sconce swelled back to amber. The window went still. The quill settled into its usual half-seen state on the blanket.

Gavin wiped his nose and looked at the quill. The wards had thinned again. It was not enough for anyone else in Hufflepuff to wake up and say the room felt wrong. It was not enough to leave a mark you could point at tomorrow morning. But the room's magic had registered it, and the first thing he'd changed had registered it worst of all.

He cleaned the handkerchief with a Scourgify that mostly worked — his wand still did ordinary spells, and he was still mediocre at them — and the worst of the blood vanished, leaving a faint pinkish ghost on the cotton. Then he set the quill on the nightstand instead of putting it back in his pocket. If the wards shifted again, he wanted the loosened thing where he could see what happened to it.

He turned on his side. Through the round window he could see the January dark, the grounds invisible except for the faint light from Hagrid's cabin far below and the deeper black of the Forest beyond it, its edge just visible against the sky. The quill sat on the nightstand, not quite holding still in the sconce-light. He watched it until sleep took him.
